name: ratatouille
instructions:
TWO PARALLEL TRACKS, COMBINED AT THE END:

1. BASE (skillet): sautee yellow onions and bell peppers in olive oil. add canned tomatoes and tomato paste, simmer down into a thick tomato sauce. season with black pepper, fennel powder, fennel seeds (crushed in a mortar), and tarragon.

2. VEGETABLES (separate skillet): sautee eggplant and zucchini SEPARATELY from the base. once cooked through, pat them dry — you want their meat, not their juices. soggy eggplant/zucchini ruins the dish.

3. COMBINE: lay the cooked eggplant and zucchini on top of the tomato sauce at the end. this is the standard ratatouille technique — the vegetables sit on top, they don't simmer in the sauce.
notes:
TOMATO FORM: canned tomatoes + tomato paste is the current path — opinion is that this works better than raw tomatoes for getting the concentrated sauce the dish wants. WANT TO TRY: whole fresh tomatoes someday as a cleaner direction (in want_to_try). NO vinegar — definitely don't add vinegar.

ONIONS: yellow onions are the canonical choice. shallots can substitute and work surprisingly well — this is one of the better uses for them.

BELL PEPPERS: optional. the fennel-bulb + fennel-powder + fennel-seed path tastes more refined and is worth trying as a bell-pepper-free variant. when using bell peppers, julia child recommends green bell peppers over red/yellow/orange — green's more vegetal/grassy character holds up against the tomato better than the sweetness of the colored varieties.

FENNEL SEEDS: best crushed in a mortar. crushed fennel seeds give the dish a nice texture (unlike in a soup, where that texture isn't welcome). fennel powder works too if you don't want to mortar.

GARLIC IS A CLASH (CONTRARY TO POPULAR LOGIC): most ratatouille recipes go heavy on garlic. i think garlic + tomato + eggplant pushes the dish too italian and erases what makes ratatouille distinct from a vegetable parmigiana or caponata. EXCEPTION: a head of garlic roasted whole in a skillet at the end as a separate component (eaten alongside, not stirred in) is fine.

SERVE WITH WHITE RICE: this dish tastes best with white rice rather than bread — unusual for a western dish (the other category that prefers rice over bread is curries). bread soaks up the sauce wrong; rice catches and holds it.
